museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: April is ports, delayed projects and late-month PlayStation weight.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight notable releases, led by month-specific anchors where possible.

items.title: Starfield
items.body: Bethesda's space RPG crosses into PlayStation territory.

items.title: Pokemon Champions
items.body: Pokemon's competitive side gets a current-platform date.

items.title: Pragmata
items.platform: PC / PS5 / Xbox Series X|S / Switch 2
items.body: Capcom's delayed sci-fi project finally becomes a release object.

items.title: Saros
items.body: Housemarque's post-Returnal project gives April a prestige PlayStation endpoint.

items.title: Animal Crossing: New Horizons Switch 2 Edition
items.body: A 2020 lockdown object is re-shelved for Switch 2.

items.title: 2XKO
items.body: Riot's fighter enters the early-year release calendar.

items.title: Final Fantasy VII Remake Intergrade
items.body: Square's remake project continues spreading across platforms.

items.title: Code Vein 2
items.body: Bandai Namco gives January a dark action-RPG shelf marker.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Platform, subscription and buying context around the month.

items.title: Switch 2 enters its second calendar year
items.body: Ports, editions and Nintendo-first releases now test whether the new machine has become ordinary.

items.title: GTA VI controls the industry's weather without launching
items.body: The moved May date leaves a visible gap in May 2026 and shapes how other games are discussed.

items.title: Xbox widens its platform map
items.body: Forza Horizon 6 launches on Xbox/PC with PS5 noted for later, underlining a less exclusive console landscape.
items.sourceNote: Gematsu and PC Gamer Forza reporting.

items.title: Online-first design remains a default assumption
items.body: Marathon, 2XKO, early access and live-service updates make network presence part of the monthly archive.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: How players discovered, argued about and shared the month.

items.title: Delay culture is communal
items.body: GTA VI's missing May date becomes memes, mourning and calendar archaeology.

items.title: Early access is part of the main shelf
items.body: Slay the Spire 2 and Subnautica 2 show how unfinished-but-public games can still feel like major dates.

items.title: Platform identity is blurrier
items.body: Ports and cross-platform releases make the old console-war map harder to read.

items.title: The archive is live while it is happening
items.body: For recent months, source notes matter because facts are still settling into history.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A short atmospheric reading from the player side of recent history.

items.title: The year began under a shadow
items.body: Even before release, GTA VI shaped conversation like a planet just offstage.

items.title: Switch 2 became normal very quickly
items.body: Ports and editions made the new console feel less like spectacle and more like furniture.

items.title: Online games felt provisional
items.body: Early access, live-service bets and post-launch roadmaps made finishedness harder to pin down.

items.title: The present already feels archival
items.body: Because these months are so recent, the exhibit feels less nostalgic and more like preserving wet paint.
